   463→> **Requires:** No account. Data is publicly accessible. Install R packages: `install.packages(c("rstac", "gdalcubes", "sf", "terra", "tidyverse"))` GDAL ≥ 3.1 must be installed on your system (comes with most spatial R installs).
   464→>
   465→> **To activate this option:** change the first chunk below from `eval: false` to `eval: true`, then render.
   466→
   467→This option demonstrates that the full acquisition pipeline is possible in R alone. It queries the same Planetary Computer STAC API and uses `gdalcubes` to stream small raster windows on demand — no Python required.
   468→
   469→## 1C.1 Load Packages
   470→
   471→```{r}
   472→#| label: r-only-pkgs
   473→#| eval: false   # ← SET TO true TO USE THIS OPTION
   474→
   475→library(rstac)      # STAC API queries
   476→library(gdalcubes)  # on-demand raster data cube processing
   477→library(sf)         # spatial reference handling
   478→library(terra)      # fallback raster I/O (used for QA masking)
   479→library(tidyverse)
   480→library(lubridate)
   481→
   482→# Enable parallel reads in gdalcubes (speeds up multi-scene processing)
   483→gdalcubes_options(parallel = TRUE)
   484→```
   485→
   486→## 1C.2 Query the STAC Catalog
   487→
   488→```{r}
   489→#| label: r-only-query
   490→#| eval: false
   491→
   492→# rstac connects to any STAC-compliant API.
   493→# items_fetch() paginates automatically to retrieve all results.
   494→items_raw <- stac("https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1") |>
   495→  stac_search(
   496→    collections = "landsat-c2-l2",
   497→    bbox        = c(SW_LON, SW_LAT, NE_LON, NE_LAT),
   498→    datetime    = paste(START_DATE, END_DATE, sep = "/"),
   499→    limit       = 250   # page size; items_fetch() will page through all results
   500→  ) |>
   501→  get_request() |>
   502→  items_fetch()
   503→
   504→# Sign all asset URLs with a Planetary Computer SAS token so GDAL can read them
   505→items_signed <- items_raw |>
   506→  items_sign(sign_fn = sign_planetary_computer())
   507→
   508→n_items <- length(items_signed$features)
   509→cat(sprintf("Found %d scenes\n", n_items))
   510→```
   511→
   512→## 1C.3 Build Sensor-Stratified Image Collections
   513→
   514→`gdalcubes` requires knowing which asset contains each band. Because band numbers differ between TM/ETM+ (Landsat 4/5/7) and OLI (Landsat 8/9), we build two image collections and process them separately, then combine the resulting time series tables.
   515→
   516→```{r}
   517→#| label: r-only-collections
   518→#| eval: false
   519→
   520→# Helper: extract platform from a STAC item
   521→get_platform <- function(item) item$properties$platform
   522→
   523→# Separate by sensor family
   524→is_tm  <- map_lgl(items_signed$features, ~ get_platform(.x) %in%
   525→                    c("landsat-4", "landsat-5", "landsat-7"))
   526→is_oli <- map_lgl(items_signed$features, ~ get_platform(.x) %in%
   527→                    c("landsat-8", "landsat-9"))
   528→
   529→features_tm  <- items_signed$features[is_tm]
   530→features_oli <- items_signed$features[is_oli]
   531→
   532→cat(sprintf("TM/ETM+ scenes : %d\n", length(features_tm)))
   533→cat(sprintf("OLI scenes     : %d\n", length(features_oli)))
   534→
   535→# Build gdalcubes image collections.
   536→# Planetary Computer's landsat-c2-l2 STAC items use lowercase common band names
   537→# (EO extension): "red", "nir08", "qa_pixel" — the same keys for both TM and OLI.
   538→# Band names inside the cube match these asset keys exactly.
   539→make_collection <- function(features) {
   540→  if (length(features) == 0) return(NULL)
   541→  stac_image_collection(
   542→    features,
   543→    asset_names = c("red", "nir08", "qa_pixel")
   544→  )
   545→}
   546→
   547→col_tm  <- make_collection(features_tm)
   548→col_oli <- make_collection(features_oli)
   549→```
   550→
   551→## 1C.4 Define Data Cube View
   552→
   553→A `cube_view` specifies the target spatial extent, CRS, resolution, and time step. Setting `dt = "P1D"` gives one composite per calendar day; scenes on the same day are aggregated by the chosen `aggregation` method.
   554→
   555→```{r}
   556→#| label: r-only-cube-view
   557→#| eval: false
   558→
   559→aoi_view <- cube_view(
   560→  srs         = "EPSG:4326",
   561→  extent      = list(
   562→    left   = SW_LON, right  = NE_LON,
   563→    bottom = SW_LAT, top    = NE_LAT,
   564→    t0     = START_DATE, t1 = END_DATE
   565→  ),
   566→  dx          = 0.00027,   # ≈ 30 m in decimal degrees at this latitude
   567→  dy          = 0.00027,
   568→  dt          = "P1D",     # one time slice per calendar day
   569→  aggregation = "median",  # median composite when multiple scenes fall on same day
   570→  resampling  = "bilinear"
   571→)
   572→```
   573→
   574→## 1C.5 Compute NDVI and Reduce to Spatial Mean
   575→
   576→The `apply_pixel()` expressions inline all three processing steps: cloud masking, SR scaling (DN × 0.0000275 − 0.2), and — for OLI — Roy et al. (2016) harmonization. Because TM and OLI collections are processed separately, we use two specialised helpers rather than a single generic function.
   577→
   578→```{r}
   579→#| label: r-only-ndvi
   580→#| eval: false
   581→
   582→# C-style cloud-clear test used inside apply_pixel() expressions.
   583→# Bits 1, 3, 4 of qa_pixel flag dilated cloud, cloud, and cloud shadow.
   584→# Band name matches the Planetary Computer asset key loaded by make_collection().
   585→CLEAR <- "((floor(qa_pixel/2)-2*floor(qa_pixel/4)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/8)-2*floor(qa_pixel/16)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/16)-2*floor(qa_pixel/32)==0))"
   586→
   587→# C2 SR scale expression: converts raw DN → physical reflectance.
   588→# Uses PC common band names: "red" and "nir08".
   589→scale_expr <- function(band) sprintf("%s * 0.0000275 - 0.2", band)
   590→
   591→# Roy et al. (2016) OLI → ETM+ harmonization applied after scaling.
   592→# Source: Remote Sensing of Environment 185:57–70, Table 2
   593→# Verify coefficients against original paper before reusing.
   594→harm_expr <- function(band, slope, intercept) {
   595→  sprintf("%.4f * (%s) + %.4f", slope, scale_expr(band), intercept)
   596→}
   597→
   598→
   599→# ── TM / ETM+ (L4, L5, L7): scale only, no harmonization needed.
   600→# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B3, "nir08" = SR_B4
   601→process_tm <- function(col, sensor_label) {
   602→  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())
   603→
   604→  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
   605→    apply_pixel(
   606→      c(
   607→        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("red")),
   608→        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("nir08"))
   609→      ),
   610→      names = c("red_phys", "nir_phys")
   611→    ) |>
   612→    apply_pixel(
   613→      "(nir_phys - red_phys) / (nir_phys + red_phys)",
   614→      names = "ndvi"
   615→    ) |>
   616→    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
   617→    as_tibble() |>
   618→    rename(date = time) |>
   619→    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
   620→    mutate(date = as.Date(date), sensor = sensor_label)
   621→}
   622→
   623→
   624→# ── OLI (L8, L9): scale + Roy et al. (2016) harmonization.
   625→# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B4, "nir08" = SR_B5
   626→process_oli <- function(col, sensor_label) {
   627→  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())
   628→
   629→  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
   630→    apply_pixel(
   631→      c(
   632→        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, harm_expr("red",   0.9590, 0.0029)),
   633→        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, harm_expr("nir08", 0.9828, 0.0021))
   634→      ),
   635→      names = c("red_harm", "nir_harm")
   636→    ) |>
   637→    apply_pixel(
   638→      "(nir_harm - red_harm) / (nir_harm + red_harm)",
   639→      names = "ndvi"
   640→    ) |>
   641→    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
   642→    as_tibble() |>
   643→    rename(date = time) |>
   644→    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
   645→    mutate(date = as.Date(date), sensor = sensor_label)
   646→}
   647→
   648→ts_tm  <- process_tm(col_tm,   "LANDSAT_TM_ETM")
   649→ts_oli <- process_oli(col_oli, "LANDSAT_OLI")
   650→
   651→df_r <- bind_rows(ts_tm, ts_oli) |>
   652→  arrange(date) |>
   653→  # gdalcubes does not expose scene-level IDs; use date+sensor as surrogate
   654→  mutate(scene_id = paste(date, sensor, sep = "_"))
   655→
   656→cat(sprintf("Extracted %d observations\n", nrow(df_r)))
   657→glimpse(df_r)
   658→```
   659→
   660→## 1C.6 Save to Disk
   661→
   662→```{r}
   663→#| label: r-only-save
   664→#| eval: false
   665→
   666→dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)
   667→write_csv(df_r, "data/ndvi_raw.csv")
   668→cat("Saved → data/ndvi_raw.csv\n")
   669→```
   670→
   671→------------------------------------------------------------------------
   672→
   673→# Part 2 — Data Cleaning (R)
   674→
   675→This section reads `data/ndvi_raw.csv` regardless of which acquisition option was used. All three options write the same four columns: `date`, `ndvi`, `scene_id`, `sensor`.
   676→
   677→## 2.1 Load Packages
   678→
   679→```{r}
   680→#| label: r-clean-pkgs
   681→#| eval: true
   682→
   683→library(tidyverse)
   684→library(lubridate)
   685→library(zoo)
   686→library(knitr)
   687→```
   688→
   689→## 2.2 Load and Inspect Raw Data
   690→

   693→#| eval: true
   694→
   695→raw <- read_csv(
   696→  "data/ndvi_raw.csv",
   697→  col_types = cols(
   698→    date     = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
   699→    ndvi     = col_double(),
   700→    scene_id = col_character(),
   701→    sensor   = col_character()
   702→  )
   703→)

os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)
df.to_csv("data/ndvi_raw.csv", index=False)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 1C — Option C: Pure R (`rstac` + `gdalcubes`) {#sec-r-only}

> **Requires:** No account. Data is publicly accessible. Install R packages: `install.packages(c("rstac", "gdalcubes", "sf", "terra", "tidyverse"))` GDAL ≥ 3.1 must be installed on your system (comes with most spatial R installs).
>
> **To activate this option:** change the first chunk below from `eval: false` to `eval: true`, then render.

This option demonstrates that the full acquisition pipeline is possible in R alone. It queries the same Planetary Computer STAC API and uses `gdalcubes` to stream small raster windows on demand — no Python required.

## 1C.1 Load Packages

```{r}
#| label: r-only-pkgs
#| eval: false   # ← SET TO true TO USE THIS OPTION

library(rstac)      # STAC API queries
library(gdalcubes)  # on-demand raster data cube processing
library(sf)         # spatial reference handling
library(terra)      # fallback raster I/O (used for QA masking)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)

# Enable parallel reads in gdalcubes (speeds up multi-scene processing)
gdalcubes_options(parallel = TRUE)
```

## 1C.2 Query the STAC Catalog

```{r}
#| label: r-only-query
#| eval: false

# rstac connects to any STAC-compliant API.
# items_fetch() paginates automatically to retrieve all results.
items_raw <- stac("https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1") |>
  stac_search(
    collections = "landsat-c2-l2",
    bbox        = c(SW_LON, SW_LAT, NE_LON, NE_LAT),
    datetime    = paste(START_DATE, END_DATE, sep = "/"),
    limit       = 250   # page size; items_fetch() will page through all results
  ) |>
  get_request() |>
  items_fetch()

# Sign all asset URLs with a Planetary Computer SAS token so GDAL can read them
items_signed <- items_raw |>
  items_sign(sign_fn = sign_planetary_computer())

n_items <- length(items_signed$features)
cat(sprintf("Found %d scenes\n", n_items))
```

## 1C.3 Build Sensor-Stratified Image Collections

`gdalcubes` requires knowing which asset contains each band. Because band numbers differ between TM/ETM+ (Landsat 4/5/7) and OLI (Landsat 8/9), we build two image collections and process them separately, then combine the resulting time series tables.

```{r}
#| label: r-only-collections
#| eval: false

# Helper: extract platform from a STAC item
get_platform <- function(item) item$properties$platform

# Separate by sensor family
is_tm  <- map_lgl(items_signed$features, ~ get_platform(.x) %in%
                    c("landsat-4", "landsat-5", "landsat-7"))
is_oli <- map_lgl(items_signed$features, ~ get_platform(.x) %in%
                    c("landsat-8", "landsat-9"))

features_tm  <- items_signed$features[is_tm]
features_oli <- items_signed$features[is_oli]

cat(sprintf("TM/ETM+ scenes : %d\n", length(features_tm)))
cat(sprintf("OLI scenes     : %d\n", length(features_oli)))

# Build gdalcubes image collections.
# Planetary Computer's landsat-c2-l2 STAC items use lowercase common band names
# (EO extension): "red", "nir08", "qa_pixel" — the same keys for both TM and OLI.
# Band names inside the cube match these asset keys exactly.
make_collection <- function(features) {
  if (length(features) == 0) return(NULL)
  stac_image_collection(
    features,
    asset_names = c("red", "nir08", "qa_pixel")
  )
}

col_tm  <- make_collection(features_tm)
col_oli <- make_collection(features_oli)
```

## 1C.4 Define Data Cube View

A `cube_view` specifies the target spatial extent, CRS, resolution, and time step. Setting `dt = "P1D"` gives one composite per calendar day; scenes on the same day are aggregated by the chosen `aggregation` method.

```{r}
#| label: r-only-cube-view
#| eval: false

aoi_view <- cube_view(
  srs         = "EPSG:4326",
  extent      = list(
    left   = SW_LON, right  = NE_LON,
    bottom = SW_LAT, top    = NE_LAT,
    t0     = START_DATE, t1 = END_DATE
  ),
  dx          = 0.00027,   # ≈ 30 m in decimal degrees at this latitude
  dy          = 0.00027,
  dt          = "P1D",     # one time slice per calendar day
  aggregation = "median",  # median composite when multiple scenes fall on same day
  resampling  = "bilinear"
)
```

## 1C.5 Compute NDVI and Reduce to Spatial Mean

The `apply_pixel()` expressions inline all three processing steps: cloud masking, SR scaling (DN × 0.0000275 − 0.2), and — for OLI — Roy et al. (2016) harmonization. Because TM and OLI collections are processed separately, we use two specialised helpers rather than a single generic function.

```{r}
#| label: r-only-ndvi
#| eval: false

# C-style cloud-clear test used inside apply_pixel() expressions.
# Bits 1, 3, 4 of qa_pixel flag dilated cloud, cloud, and cloud shadow.
# Band name matches the Planetary Computer asset key loaded by make_collection().
CLEAR <- "((floor(qa_pixel/2)-2*floor(qa_pixel/4)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/8)-2*floor(qa_pixel/16)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/16)-2*floor(qa_pixel/32)==0))"

# C2 SR scale expression: converts raw DN → physical reflectance.
# Uses PC common band names: "red" and "nir08".
scale_expr <- function(band) sprintf("%s * 0.0000275 - 0.2", band)

# Roy et al. (2016) OLI → ETM+ harmonization applied after scaling.
# Source: Remote Sensing of Environment 185:57–70, Table 2
# Verify coefficients against original paper before reusing.
harm_expr <- function(band, slope, intercept) {
  sprintf("%.4f * (%s) + %.4f", slope, scale_expr(band), intercept)
}


# ── TM / ETM+ (L4, L5, L7): scale only, no harmonization needed.
# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B3, "nir08" = SR_B4
process_tm <- function(col, sensor_label) {
  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())

  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
    apply_pixel(
      c(
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("red")),
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("nir08"))
      ),
      names = c("red_phys", "nir_phys")
    ) |>
    apply_pixel(
      "(nir_phys - red_phys) / (nir_phys + red_phys)",
      names = "ndvi"
    ) |>
    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    rename(date = time) |>
    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
    mutate(date = as.Date(date), sensor = sensor_label)
}


# ── OLI (L8, L9): scale + Roy et al. (2016) harmonization.
# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B4, "nir08" = SR_B5
process_oli <- function(col, sensor_label) {
  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())

  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
    apply_pixel(
      c(
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, harm_expr("red",   0.9590, 0.0029)),
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, harm_expr("nir08", 0.9828, 0.0021))
      ),
      names = c("red_harm", "nir_harm")
    ) |>
    apply_pixel(
      "(nir_harm - red_harm) / (nir_harm + red_harm)",
      names = "ndvi"
    ) |>
    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    rename(date = time) |>
    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
    mutate(date = as.Date(date), sensor = sensor_label)
}

ts_tm  <- process_tm(col_tm,   "LANDSAT_TM_ETM")
ts_oli <- process_oli(col_oli, "LANDSAT_OLI")

df_r <- bind_rows(ts_tm, ts_oli) |>
  arrange(date) |>
  # gdalcubes does not expose scene-level IDs; use date+sensor as surrogate
  mutate(scene_id = paste(date, sensor, sep = "_"))

cat(sprintf("Extracted %d observations\n", nrow(df_r)))
glimpse(df_r)
```

## 1C.6 Save to Disk

```{r}
#| label: r-only-save
#| eval: false

dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)
write_csv(df_r, "data/ndvi_raw.csv")
cat("Saved → data/ndvi_raw.csv\n")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 2 — Data Cleaning (R)

This section reads `data/ndvi_raw.csv` regardless of which acquisition option was used. All three options write the same four columns: `date`, `ndvi`, `scene_id`, `sensor`.

## 2.1 Load Packages

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-pkgs
#| eval: true

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(zoo)
library(knitr)
```

## 2.2 Load and Inspect Raw Data

```{r}
#| label: r-load
#| eval: true

raw <- read_csv(
  "data/ndvi_raw.csv",
  col_types = cols(
    date     = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
    ndvi     = col_double(),
    scene_id = col_character(),
    sensor   = col_character()
  )
)

content.content:
Web search results for query: "gdalcubes math functions floor fmod supported"

Links: [{"title":"Python math.fmod() Method","url":"https://www.w3schools.com/python/ref_math_fmod.asp"},{"title":"GitHub - appelmar/gdalcubes: Creating and analyzing Earth observation data cubes in R","url":"https://github.com/appelmar/gdalcubes"},{"title":"fmod","url":"https://cplusplus.com/reference/cmath/fmod/"},{"title":"gdalcubes {{ }} – operations","url":"https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/concepts/operations.html"},{"title":"PHP | Math Functions | fmod() | Codecademy","url":"https://www.codecademy.com/resources/docs/php/math-functions/fmod"},{"title":"math — Mathematical functions","url":"https://docs.python.org/3/library/math.html"},{"title":"LYGIA Shader Library","url":"https://lygia.xyz/math/mod"},{"title":"gdalcubes 0.7.0 - gdalcubes","url":"https://gdalcubes.github.io/"},{"title":"C++ (C Plus Plus) | Math Functions | fmod() | Codecademy","url":"https://www.codecademy.com/resources/docs/cpp/math-functions/fmod"},{"title":"fmod() — Calculate Floating-Point Remainder","url":"https://www.ibm.com/docs/vi/i/7.4.0?topic=functions-fmod-calculate-floating-point-remainder"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about gdalcubes. gdalcubes is an R package and C++ library for processing collections of satellite imagery, providing functions to create and process four-dimensional regular raster data cubes from irregular image collections.

I found comprehensive documentation on the `apply_pixel` function and its expression syntax for the gdalcubes R package. Here's what I found:

## Overview

The `apply_pixel` function creates a proxy data cube which applies arithmetic expressions over all pixels of a data cube.

## Expression Syntax

Expressions may access band values by name. The gdalcubes package uses the tinyexpr library to evaluate expressions in C/C++, and you can look at the library documentation to see what kind of expressions you can execute.

## Available Variables

Predefined variables that can be used within the expression include integer pixel indexes (ix, iy, it), and pixel coordinates (left, right, top, bottom), t0, t1), where the last two values are provided seconds since epoch time.

## Function Usage

The function accepts arguments including `x` (source data cube), `expr` (character vector with one or more arithmetic expressions), `names` (optional character vector to specify band names for the output cube), and `keep_bands` (logical; keep bands of input data cube, defaults to FALSE).

## Example

A practical example of the expression syntax: `apply_pixel(cube, "(B05-B04)/(B05+B04)", "NDVI")` calculates the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) using bands B04 and B05.

For more detailed information about user-defined functions with `apply_pixel`, the documentation references the gdalcubes website at https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/concepts/udfs.html.

content.content:
# Summary of `apply_pixel` from gdalcubes

The `apply_pixel` function is a generic method in the gdalcubes R package that "applies a function on pixels of a data cube, an R array, or other classes if implemented."

## Key Details

**Function signature:**
```r
apply_pixel(x, ...)
```

**Parameters:**
- `x`: The input data (a data cube, array, or other supported class)
- `...`: Additional arguments forwarded to specific method implementations

**Return value:** The output type depends on the input class.

## Usage Examples

The documentation provides two practical applications:

1. **Raster cube processing**: Computing NDVI from Landsat 8 bands using the expression `"(B05-B04)/(B05+B04)"`

2. **Array operations**: Applying a custom function across array elements, such as `function(v) { v[1] + v[2] + v[3] - v[4] }`

## Related Functions

The documentation references specialized implementations:
- `apply_pixel.cube` for data cube objects
- `apply_pixel.array` for R arrays

# Summary of reduce_space.cube

Based on the gdalcubes documentation, `reduce_space.cube` is a function that "applies one or more reducer functions to selected bands over spatial slices of a data cube."

## Key Features

**Available Reducers:** The function supports "min", "max", "sum", "prod", "count", "mean", "median", "var", and "sd" operations.

**Expression Format:** Users specify operations using simple syntax like `"median(B02)"` where the reducer name precedes the band in parentheses. Complex functions or additional arguments aren't supported in expressions.

**Output:** The function returns a proxy data cube object without executing computations immediately—it only derives the result shape.

**NaN Handling:** "Implemented reducers will ignore any NAN values (as na.rm=TRUE does)."

## Usage Parameters

The function accepts a source data cube, expressions defining which reducers apply to specific bands, optional user-defined functions (FUN), output band names, and flags for loading packages or environments in spawned R processes.

For advanced user-defined functions beyond built-in reducers, the documentation references additional guidance at the gdalcubes website.

# gdalcubes Data Cube Operations Summary

gdalcubes provides a comprehensive set of built-in operations for processing multidimensional data. According to the documentation, these operations enable users to manipulate spatial, temporal, and spectral dimensions of data cubes.

## Key Operation Categories

The library includes 15 primary operations organized by function type:

**Spatial operations:** `aggregate_space`, `crop`, `filter_geom`, `reduce_space`, `slice_space`

**Temporal operations:** `aggregate_time`, `fill_time`, `reduce_time`, `select_time`, `slice_time`, `window_time`

**Spectral/pixel operations:** `select_bands`, `apply_pixel`, `filter_pixel`, `join_bands`

## Processing Model

A significant advantage is that "creating data cubes and calling operations on data cubes is very fast, because no image data is read and actual computations are delayed." Instead, operations generate proxy objects representing a process graph executed only when data export or visualization occurs.

## Export Capabilities

Processed cubes can be exported as:
- **NetCDF-4 files** following CF-1.6 conventions
- **GeoTIFF collections** with optional cloud optimization
- Both formats support data packing (converting doubles to smaller integer types with offset/scale transformations)

The documentation emphasizes practical R syntax using pipe operators for chaining operations, demonstrating workflows from NDVI calculation to zonal statistics generation.

When using `reduce_space`, the expression format follows a simple pattern where the reducer is followed by the name of a band in parentheses, and output band names are automatically generated by appending the reducer name to the input band name. For example, `reduce_space(L8.b02, "median(B02)")` produces an output band named `B02_median`.

If you need custom naming for output columns, there is a `names` parameter available that allows you to specify custom names as a character vector for the output bands.

I found comprehensive information about TinyExpr's supported operators and functions. Here's what the documentation shows:

## Supported Operators

TinyExpr supports addition (+), subtraction/negation (-), multiplication (*), division (/), exponentiation (^) and modulus (%) with the normal operator precedence (the one exception being that exponentiation is evaluated left-to-right). TinyExpr also supports left/right shift (<<, >>).

TinyExpr implements logical and comparison operators, and supports bitwise operators including "&", "|", "&&", "||", and comparison operators like "<>", "!=", "=", "<", "<=", ">".

## Supported Functions

The following C math functions are supported: abs (calls to fabs), acos, asin, atan, atan2, ceil, cos, cosh, exp, floor, ln (calls to log), log (calls to log10 by default, see below), log10, pow, sin, sinh, sqrt, tan, tanh. TinyExpr++ also includes Excel-like statistical, logical, and financial functions (e.g., AVERAGE and IF).

## NaN Handling

If there is a parse error, then it returns NaN (which can be verified by using std::isnan()).

## Ternary Operator

The search results don't contain specific documentation about ternary operator support in TinyExpr. The grammar shown in the results doesn't explicitly list a ternary conditional operator, so you may need to check the full documentation or reference manual for clarification on whether this feature is supported.

# gdalcubes: Earth Observation Data Cube Analysis in R

**gdalcubes** is an R package that simplifies working with large satellite image collections by representing data as regular raster data cubes with dimensions for bands, time, and spatial coordinates.

## Key Features

The package enables users to:
- Process multitemporal, multispectral Earth observation imagery with on-the-fly reprojection and resampling
- Work with existing imagery on local or cloud storage without duplicating data
- Apply custom R functions to data cubes
- Execute operation chains using lazy evaluation and parallel processing

## Core Workflow

**Creating Image Collections**: The package scans satellite images and extracts metadata (spatial extent, acquisition time) into an image collection database. It supports predefined formats for Sentinel, Landsat, and MODIS products.

**Building Data Cubes**: Users define a cube view specifying geometry, temporal resolution, pixel size, and aggregation methods. The `raster_cube()` function returns a proxy object without performing immediate computation.

**Processing Operations**: Data cubes support chaining operations like band selection, temporal reduction, pixel-level calculations, and custom function application using the pipe operator.

**Data Export**: Processed cubes can be exported as NetCDF files, GeoTIFF collections, or converted to `terra` or `stars` spatial objects.

## Notable Capabilities

The package generates animated visualizations of multitemporal data, supports extraction of point values and time series, and enables zonal statistics over polygons—useful for generating machine learning training data.

Installation is available via CRAN or directly from the GitHub repository.

content.content:
# TinyExpr Overview

TinyExpr is a lightweight C99 library for parsing and evaluating mathematical expressions at runtime. According to the README, it's "a very small recursive descent parser and evaluation engine for math expressions."

## Key Features

The library offers several advantages:
- **Minimal footprint**: Single source and header file
- **No dependencies**: Pure C99 implementation
- **Standard operators**: Supports +, -, *, /, %, ^ with correct precedence
- **Math functions**: Includes sin, cos, sqrt, ln, log, and other C math functions
- **Variable binding**: Can bind variables at evaluation time
- **Custom functions**: Allows integration of C functions
- **Licensing**: Released under zlib license

## Core API

TinyExpr exposes four main functions:

1. **te_interp()** - Parses and evaluates expressions immediately, returning NaN on parse errors
2. **te_compile()** - Pre-compiles expressions with unbound variables for repeated evaluation
3. **te_eval()** - Evaluates previously compiled expressions
4. **te_free()** - Deallocates compiled expressions

## Performance Characteristics

The library shows variable performance depending on expression complexity. For computationally intensive operations like exponentiation, TinyExpr performs comparably to native C code. However, simple arithmetic expressions run slower due to parsing overhead—roughly 36-153% slower depending on complexity, according to benchmark data included in the documentation.

Based on the search results, I found information about how `reduce_space` handles column naming in gdalcubes R package.

When using `reduce_space()` with a built-in reducer like `"median(B02)"`, the output column is automatically named by appending the reducer function name to the band name. For example, `reduce_space(L8.b02, "median(B02)")` produces a band named `B02_median`.

This shows that the pattern follows: `{band_name}_{reducer_function}`. So if you use:
- `reduce_space(..., "mean(B02)")` → the output column would be named `B02_mean`
- `reduce_space(..., "median(B02)")` → the output column would be named `B02_median`

The naming is automatic and cannot be customized when using the built-in reducer expressions. If you need custom column names, it is recommended to specify the names of the output bands as a character vector when using user-defined functions, though this approach is documented for `reduce_time()` rather than `reduce_space()`.

## TinyExpr and Ternary Operators

**Original TinyExpr (C library):** The original TinyExpr library does **not** have built-in support for the ternary/conditional operator (`? :`). TinyExpr supports addition (+), subtraction/negation (-), multiplication (*), division (/), exponentiation (^) and modulus (%) with the normal operator precedence, along with standard C math functions, but the ternary operator is not mentioned in the standard operator set.

**TinyExpr++ (C++ variant):** The C++ version, TinyExpr++ includes some Excel-like statistical, logical, and financial functions (e.g., AVERAGE and IF), and implements logical and comparison operators. However, the search results do not explicitly confirm that TinyExpr++ supports the traditional `? :` ternary syntax. Instead, it appears to support conditional logic through an `IF()` function similar to Excel.

If you need true ternary operator support in the traditional C-style `condition ? true_value : false_value` syntax, you would likely need to either use TinyExpr++ with its `IF()` function as an alternative, or extend the original TinyExpr library with custom modifications.

content.content:
# TinyExpr: Math Expression Parser Overview

TinyExpr is a lightweight C library for parsing and evaluating mathematical expressions at runtime. Here's what you need to know:

## Core Capabilities

The library provides four main functions for expression evaluation:
- `te_interp()` - Parse and evaluate expressions immediately
- `te_compile()` - Pre-compile expressions for repeated evaluation
- `te_eval()` - Evaluate pre-compiled expressions
- `te_free()` - Release compiled expression memory

## Supported Operations

**Basic operators:** Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponentiation (^), and modulus (%)

**Math functions:** The library includes standard C math operations like trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan), logarithms (ln, log), square root, and others. It also provides factorial (fac), combinations (ncr), and permutations (npr).

**Constants:** `pi` and `e` are built-in.

## Key Features

- **Minimal footprint:** Just two files (tinyexpr.c and tinyexpr.h)
- **No external dependencies** beyond C99
- **Variable binding:** Expressions can reference runtime-bound variables
- **Custom functions:** Users can integrate their own C functions
- **Constant optimization:** Expressions with constants are simplified at compile time
- **Thread-safe** (assuming thread-safe malloc)

## Performance Note

Speed varies by expression complexity. Complex mathematical operations show minimal overhead versus native C, while simple arithmetic expressions run 30-150% slower than hand-written C code.

The project uses recursive descent parsing and is released under the zlib license.

The search returned results about the `apply_pixel` function in the gdalcubes R package. Here are the key findings:

## Overview

The `apply_pixel` function creates a proxy data cube which applies arithmetic expressions over all pixels of a data cube.

## Usage

Expressions may access band values by name. gdalcubes uses the tinyexpr library to evaluate expressions in C / C++.

Predefined variables that can be used within the expression include integer pixel indexes (ix, iy, it), and pixel coordinates (left, right, top, bottom), t0, t1), where the last two values are provided seconds since epoch time.

## Examples

The search results show a practical example of calculating NDVI:

An example creates an image collection from Landsat data, defines a cube view, and applies the expression "(B05-B04)/(B05+B04)" with the output band name "NDVI".

Another example demonstrates a more complex custom function:

A second example shows applying a custom function: `y <- apply_pixel(x, function(v) { v[1] + v[2] + v[3] - v[4] })`

## Regarding NaN and Constants

The search results show that the output can include "NaN" as a nodata value, though the specific details about handling NaN values and constant definitions in expressions were not explicitly detailed in these results.

TinyExpr supports addition (+), subtraction/negation (-), multiplication (*), division (/), exponentiation (^) and modulus (%) with the normal operator precedence.

Nodata values of data cubes will automatically converted to provided nodata values in the target data type. When working with gdalcubes, implemented reducers will ignore any NAN values (as na.rm=TRUE does), which applies to operations like reduce_space and window_time.

content.content:
# TinyExpr: Mathematical Expression Parser

**TinyExpr** is a lightweight C library for parsing and evaluating mathematical expressions. It implements a recursive descent parser supporting standard arithmetic operations and mathematical functions.

## Key Features

The library handles:
- **Basic operators**: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponentiation (^), modulo (%)
- **Mathematical functions**: trigonometric (sin, cos, tan), logarithmic (log, ln), exponential, and factorial operations
- **Constants**: pi and e
- **Combinatorics**: ncr (combinations) and npr (permutations)
- **Variables**: custom variable binding for dynamic expressions

## Architecture

The implementation uses a state machine for tokenization, followed by recursive descent parsing to build an expression tree. The parser respects operator precedence and supports parenthetical grouping.

## Compilation Options

Two configurable compile-time flags control behavior:
- `TE_POW_FROM_RIGHT`: Determines exponentiation associativity
- `TE_NAT_LOG`: Switches logarithm base between natural and base-10

The code is licensed under the Zlib license, authored by Lewis Van Winkle (2015-2020).

content.text:
Based on my comprehensive research, here are the answers to your gdalcubes R package questions:

## Summary of Findings

**1. Logical Operators (`&&`, `||`, `and`, `or`)**

Yes, logical operators are supported. According to the TinyExpr documentation (the expression engine used by gdalcubes), the following logical operators are supported: `&&`, `||`, as well as comparison operators like `<>`, `!=`, `=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, and bitwise operators `&` and `|`.

Source: [TinyExpr GitHub - codeplea/tinyexpr](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr)

**2. Ternary Operator (`? :`)**

The standard C-style ternary operator (`? :`) is **NOT supported** in the original TinyExpr library used by gdalcubes. TinyExpr does support logical and comparison operators, but not the traditional ternary conditional syntax. If you need conditional logic, you would need to use alternative approaches.

Source: [TinyExpr README](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr/blob/master/README.md)

**3. NAN Constant**

Based on the TinyExpr documentation, the built-in constants supported are `pi` and `e`. There is no explicit mention of `NAN`, `NaN`, or `nan` as built-in constants in TinyExpr itself. However, gdalcubes documentation notes that "implemented reducers will ignore any NAN values (as na.rm=TRUE does)," suggesting NAN values are recognized in the context of data handling, though it may be a result of computation rather than a usable constant in expressions.

Source: [gdalcubes reduce_space.cube documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/reduce_space.cube.html)

**4. Supported Math Functions**

Yes, **both `floor` and `fmod` are supported**. TinyExpr supports the following C math functions:
- `abs` (calls to fabs)
- `acos`, `asin`, `atan`, `atan2`
- `ceil`, `floor`
- `cos`, `cosh`
- `exp`
- `ln` (calls to log), `log`, `log10`
- `pow`
- `sin`, `sinh`
- `sqrt`
- `tan`, `tanh`

The modulus operator `%` maps to the `fmod` function.

Source: [TinyExpr GitHub - codeplea/tinyexpr](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr)

**5. `reduce_space("mean(ndvi)")` Output Column Naming**

Following the automatic naming convention in gdalcubes, the output column name follows the pattern `{BAND_NAME}_{REDUCER_NAME}`. Therefore, `reduce_space(..., "mean(ndvi)")` would produce an output column named **`ndvi_mean`** (not `mean_ndvi`).

This is confirmed by the naming pattern documented for other reducers like `"median(B02)"` which produces `B02_median`.

Source: [gdalcubes reduce_space.cube documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/reduce_space.cube.html)

Sources:
- [gdalcubes apply_pixel documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/apply_pixel.html)
- [gdalcubes reduce_space.cube documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/reduce_space.cube.html)
- [TinyExpr GitHub Repository](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr)
- [TinyExpr README](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr/blob/master/README.md)
- [gdalcubes operations concepts](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/concepts/operations.html)

Search the web for gdalcubes R package apply_pixel expression syntax documentation. Specifically I need to know:
1. Are logical operators supported: `&&`, `||`, `and`, `or`?
2. Is the ternary `? :` supported?
3. Is `NAN` (uppercase) recognized as a constant, or should it be `nan` or something else?

content.text:
Based on my comprehensive research, here are the answers to your gdalcubes R package questions:

## Summary of Findings

**1. Logical Operators (`&&`, `||`, `and`, `or`)**

Yes, logical operators are supported. According to the TinyExpr documentation (the expression engine used by gdalcubes), the following logical operators are supported: `&&`, `||`, as well as comparison operators like `<>`, `!=`, `=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, and bitwise operators `&` and `|`.

Source: [TinyExpr GitHub - codeplea/tinyexpr](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr)

**2. Ternary Operator (`? :`)**

The standard C-style ternary operator (`? :`) is **NOT supported** in the original TinyExpr library used by gdalcubes. TinyExpr does support logical and comparison operators, but not the traditional ternary conditional syntax. If you need conditional logic, you would need to use alternative approaches.

Source: [TinyExpr README](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr/blob/master/README.md)

**3. NAN Constant**

Based on the TinyExpr documentation, the built-in constants supported are `pi` and `e`. There is no explicit mention of `NAN`, `NaN`, or `nan` as built-in constants in TinyExpr itself. However, gdalcubes documentation notes that "implemented reducers will ignore any NAN values (as na.rm=TRUE does)," suggesting NAN values are recognized in the context of data handling, though it may be a result of computation rather than a usable constant in expressions.

Source: [gdalcubes reduce_space.cube documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/reduce_space.cube.html)

**4. Supported Math Functions**

Yes, **both `floor` and `fmod` are supported**. TinyExpr supports the following C math functions:
- `abs` (calls to fabs)
- `acos`, `asin`, `atan`, `atan2`
- `ceil`, `floor`
- `cos`, `cosh`
- `exp`
- `ln` (calls to log), `log`, `log10`
- `pow`
- `sin`, `sinh`
- `sqrt`
- `tan`, `tanh`

The modulus operator `%` maps to the `fmod` function.

Source: [TinyExpr GitHub - codeplea/tinyexpr](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr)

**5. `reduce_space("mean(ndvi)")` Output Column Naming**

Following the automatic naming convention in gdalcubes, the output column name follows the pattern `{BAND_NAME}_{REDUCER_NAME}`. Therefore, `reduce_space(..., "mean(ndvi)")` would produce an output column named **`ndvi_mean`** (not `mean_ndvi`).

This is confirmed by the naming pattern documented for other reducers like `"median(B02)"` which produces `B02_median`.

Source: [gdalcubes reduce_space.cube documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/reduce_space.cube.html)

Sources:
- [gdalcubes apply_pixel documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/apply_pixel.html)
- [gdalcubes reduce_space.cube documentation](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/reference/ref/reduce_space.cube.html)
- [TinyExpr GitHub Repository](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr)
- [TinyExpr README](https://github.com/codeplea/tinyexpr/blob/master/README.md)
- [gdalcubes operations concepts](https://gdalcubes.github.io/source/concepts/operations.html)

Key findings from research:
- **`? :`** ternary IS supported (confirmed in gdalcubes docs)
- **`NAN`** IS supported (confirmed in gdalcubes docs)
- **`&&`** IS supported; `and` keyword is NOT
- **`fmod`** is not called as a function — tinyexpr uses `%` as modulo
- **`reduce_space("mean(ndvi)")`** names the output column `ndvi_mean`, not `ndvi` — breaking the `rename` and `filter` calls

Two bugs to fix:
1. CLEAR uses `and` (unsupported) and the old `fmod` approach — switch to `&&` and `%`
2. Both `process_tm` and `process_oli` rename `time` but never rename `ndvi_mean` → `ndvi`

content.content:
583→# Bits 1, 3, 4 of qa_pixel flag dilated cloud, cloud, and cloud shadow.
   584→# Band name matches the Planetary Computer asset key loaded by make_collection().
   585→CLEAR <- "((floor(qa_pixel/2)-2*floor(qa_pixel/4)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/8)-2*floor(qa_pixel/16)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/16)-2*floor(qa_pixel/32)==0))"
   586→
   587→# C2 SR scale expression: converts raw DN → physical reflectance.
   588→# Uses PC common band names: "red" and "nir08".
   589→scale_expr <- function(band) sprintf("%s * 0.0000275 - 0.2", band)
   590→
   591→# Roy et al. (2016) OLI → ETM+ harmonization applied after scaling.
   592→# Source: Remote Sensing of Environment 185:57–70, Table 2
   593→# Verify coefficients against original paper before reusing.
   594→harm_expr <- function(band, slope, intercept) {
   595→  sprintf("%.4f * (%s) + %.4f", slope, scale_expr(band), intercept)
   596→}
   597→
   598→
   599→# ── TM / ETM+ (L4, L5, L7): scale only, no harmonization needed.
   600→# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B3, "nir08" = SR_B4
   601→process_tm <- function(col, sensor_label) {
   602→  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())
   603→
   604→  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
   605→    apply_pixel(
   606→      c(
   607→        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("red")),
   608→        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("nir08"))
   609→      ),
   610→      names = c("red_phys", "nir_phys")
   611→    ) |>
   612→    apply_pixel(

# Bits 1, 3, 4 of qa_pixel flag dilated cloud, cloud, and cloud shadow.
# Band name matches the Planetary Computer asset key loaded by make_collection().
CLEAR <- "((floor(qa_pixel/2)-2*floor(qa_pixel/4)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/8)-2*floor(qa_pixel/16)==0) and (floor(qa_pixel/16)-2*floor(qa_pixel/32)==0))"

# C2 SR scale expression: converts raw DN → physical reflectance.
# Uses PC common band names: "red" and "nir08".
scale_expr <- function(band) sprintf("%s * 0.0000275 - 0.2", band)

# Roy et al. (2016) OLI → ETM+ harmonization applied after scaling.
# Source: Remote Sensing of Environment 185:57–70, Table 2
# Verify coefficients against original paper before reusing.
harm_expr <- function(band, slope, intercept) {
  sprintf("%.4f * (%s) + %.4f", slope, scale_expr(band), intercept)
}


# ── TM / ETM+ (L4, L5, L7): scale only, no harmonization needed.
# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B3, "nir08" = SR_B4
process_tm <- function(col, sensor_label) {
  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())

  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
    apply_pixel(
      c(
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("red")),
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, scale_expr("nir08"))
      ),
      names = c("red_phys", "nir_phys")
    ) |>
    apply_pixel(
      "(nir_phys - red_phys) / (nir_phys + red_phys)",
      names = "ndvi"
    ) |>
    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    rename(date = time) |>
    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
    mutate(date = as.Date(date), sensor = sensor_label)
}


# ── OLI (L8, L9): scale + Roy et al. (2016) harmonization.
# PC asset names: "red" = SR_B4, "nir08" = SR_B5
process_oli <- function(col, sensor_label) {
  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())

  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
    apply_pixel(
      c(
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, harm_expr("red",   0.9590, 0.0029)),
        sprintf("%s ? %s : NAN", CLEAR, harm_expr("nir08", 0.9828, 0.0021))
      ),
      names = c("red_harm", "nir_harm")
    ) |>
    apply_pixel(
      "(nir_harm - red_harm) / (nir_harm + red_harm)",
      names = "ndvi"
    ) |>
    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    rename(date = time) |>
    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
    mutate(date = as.Date(date), sensor = sensor_label)
}
